title: CMP, Condition Codes, and B.NE
lesson:
MIPS branches read registers directly (bne $a, $b, label). ARM splits the comparison and the branch into two steps. 'cmp x1, x2' subtracts x2 from x1 and sets four condition flags (N, Z, C, V), without actually storing the result anywhere. A subsequent 'b.ne label' (branch if not equal) reads the Z flag and jumps if it's clear.

This is the family of flags you'll meet again on every CISC and most RISCs (x86 has EFLAGS, the 6502 had its own status register, the LC-3 has N/Z/P, the PDP-8 had Link). The pattern is the same everywhere: a compare or arithmetic op sets the flags, then a conditional branch reads them.

The countdown loop on ARM looks almost identical to MIPS: load a counter, do work, decrement, compare to zero, branch back while not equal.
prompt: Compute 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 (= 15) with a counted loop. Counter in x1, running sum in x2. Each pass: add the counter to the sum, subtract 1 from the counter, cmp with 0, b.ne back. Then print the sum.
starterCode:
.text
        mov x1, #5           // counter
        mov x2, #0           // sum
loop:
        add x2, x2, x1
        sub x1, x1, #1
        cmp x1, #0
        b.ne loop
        mov x0, x2           // copy sum to x0 for printing
        mov x16, #1
        svc #0
        mov x16, #93
        svc #0
hints: cmp x1, #0 sets the Z flag if x1 is zero.
b.ne loop branches as long as Z is clear, so the loop ends when x1 reaches 0.
After the loop, move the sum into x0 and print with x16 = 1 + svc #0.
reference:
.text
mov x1, #5
mov x2, #0
loop:
add x2, x2, x1
sub x1, x1, #1
cmp x1, #0
b.ne loop
mov x0, x2
mov x16, #1
svc #0
mov x16, #93
svc #0
